California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 11, 1991
John c. Barisone
city Attorney
City of Santa cruz
333 Church Street
Santa Cruz, CA 95050
Re: Your Request For fnforrnal Advice
Our File No. I-90-775
Dear Mr. Barisone:
You have requested advice on behalf of santa cruz city
Councilperson Scott Kennedy concerning the application of the
confl ict-of- interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the
rrnql'r1.1 Because your questions do not pertai-n to a specific
pending^dec i s ion, we are treating your request as one for informal
advice . z
OUESTTONS
Mr. Kennedy is an employee of the Eschaton Foundation'
Eschaton has Loined funds to the comtnunity Housing Corporation
("CHC") for a development in downtown Santa Cruz.
l-. 
wouLd Mr. Kennedy have a conflict of interest if he
participates in city counsel decisions pertaining to the -
bevelopirent of the particular project for which Eschaton lent CHC
funds (the rtEschaton loan projectrr)?
2. 
would Mr. Kennedy have a conflict of interest if he
participates in decisions pertaining to the devel'opment of other
cHc projects?
1 Go.r"a.,."nt Code Sections 81OOo-91o15. A11 statutory references
are to trle covernment code unless otherwise indicated. 
cornrnission
iegutations appear at 2 California Code of Regutations. Section
ie6oo, et seq.- AI1 references to regutations are to Title 2,
oivision o of tn" calitornia code of Regulations'
2 
Infot.aL assistance does not provide the requestor wj'th the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice' (section
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, suite 8oo . P.o. Box 8O? . sacramento cA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) 322'5660


our FiIe No.
Page 2
I-90-775
3. 
once Eschaton's relation to cHc as a creditor has
terminated, will Mr. Kennedy have a conflict of interest if he
participates in future decisions pertaining to other cHc projects?
CONCLUSIONS
I & 2 shoul-d the decisions foreseeably cause Eschaton to
incur additional expenses of 925,000 or more or cause an increase
or decrease of Eschaton gross receipts for the fiscal year to be
$100,000 or nore, Mr. Kennedy would have a conflict of interest.
3. 
Absent other facts, Mr. Kennedy would not have a
conflict of interest once Eschaton's relation to cHc as a creditor
has terminated.
FACTS
In addition to being a member of the Santa cruz City Council,
Mr. Kennedy is a half-time employee of an organization known as
the Resource Center for Nonviolence (rtRCNv"). RclW is one of
several programs operated by the Eschaton Foundation. Rclw is
responsible for its own fundraising. These funds are segregated,
and it is from these funds that Mr. Kennedy is paid. 
Funds
generated by Eschaton are not used to compensate Mr. Kennedy.
Eschaton is a nonprofit, tax-exempt, rel-igious and educationaL
foundation based in santa cruz. Eschaton is incorporated in the
State of California and has been gTranted 501(c) (3) status by the
Internal Revenue Service. For tax purposes, Rclw is considered
under Eschaton's umbrella for corporate and non-profit status.
Eschaton's gross receipts for 1989 were $L,649,oo0.
In May 1990, Eschaton Ioaned $25o,ooo to cHc for the
acquisition and predevelopment financing of a parcel of property
in dorrrntown Santa Cruz. The loan is a short term note with a
nine-month repa)4nent period. 
The loan is at seven percent sirnple
interest and is secured by a deed of trust on the property to be
purchased .
cHc is currently developing three other housing proiects
within the city of Santa cruz. cHc thus has four projects pending
which it expects to come before the city Council for various
permits and approvals.
ANALYSIS
The Eschaton Loan Proi ect
Section 87100 prohibits any public official 
from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official 
position
to influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
Iilr. Kennedy, a member of the city councit, is
a "public off icia].'r 
(Section 82048.)
■.


Our File No. I-9O-71 5
Page 3
The Act provides a four-part test to determine whether a
public officiil 
has a conflict of interest in a particular
!o""i".""iit 
decision. First, is the official 
making, participat-
ing in rnaking, or using his official 
position to influence a
g;i"ir,n".,ta1- decision? (Section 871OO.) Second, is it reasonably
ioreseeable that the decision wil,l affect the official's 
economic
inieiest=Z (Section gZ1O3.) Third, is the effect of the decision
on the official's 
econonic interests material? (Ig') 
Fourth, is
the effect of the decision on the official,s 
econolDic interest
distinguishable frolo its effect on the public generally? (Id')
Yourlettermentionsdecisionsbythecitycouncilpertaining
to the consideration of pernj'ts and other aPprovals for the
Eschaton loan project. 
-such actions are clearly governrnental
decisions. (RLgulation 18?00(b), coPY enclosed')
B. 
Foreseeable Financial Effect
The second issue is the foreseeabilj.ty that the decision wiII
affect the official's 
economic interest' 
The paraneters of a
;;;ii. 
official's 
economic interests are set forth in sectj-on
'ezror. For the purposes of the question at hand,
An official 
has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it i's
reasonably foreseeabte that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, ' ' ' on:
***
(c) AnY source of incorne,
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($25o) or
n6ie in value provided to, received by or prornised
to the pubtic ofticj-at within 12 months prior to
the tine when the decision is made.
**rl
section 871o3.
Theeffectofadecisionisreasonablyforeseeableifthere
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur' To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibilityi 
hower". certainty is not required' 
(Downev -ceres v'
bownev Cornitunitv Develonment Com- (l'987) L96 cal' App' 3d 983,
ge9-9gr; wrE-y----UPIr"l (1977) 70 cal-. App. 3d 817')
Mr. Kennedy has received $25o from Rclw in the prior twelve
rnonths bef ore h-is decisions. Although Rclw appears to be a sel-f -
suf f ici,ent program of Eschaton, it is still 
only a subpart of
A.


Our Fi■e No.  I-90-775
Page 4
Eschaton. For tax purposes, RClw is considered under Eschaton's
umbrella for corporate and non-profit status. 
Under these
circumstances, Eschaton is a source of incorne to Mr. Kennedy'
The question is whether any decisions by l'1r. Kennedy on
approvals on the Eschaton loan project will have a foreseeable
eiiect upon Eschaton, a source of income. lvhile the commission is
not a finder of fact for the purposes of its advice letters, it
appears 1ike1y that approvaLs of the project could have a
f-oieseeable eifect on Eschaton. cHc may have great dif f ieuJ-ty
paying off the loan should the project not be completed ' 
Should
tn" city council disapprove the project and cHc become unable to
make payments on the Ioan, Eschaton could foreclose on the
mortgige, incurring costs by doing so. Eschaton could also lose
sorne receipts from interest paynents.
C. 
Materialitv
In order to determine materiality, 
we rnust first 
determine
vrhether the public official's 
economic interest is directly
invol,ved in tne decision and erhether the effect of the decision is
rnaterial under Secti.on 18702.1. If the official/s 
economic
interest i.s not directly involved in the decision, or the effect
of the decision is not rnaterial under Section 18702.1, then we
examine whether the decision indirectly affects the official's
economj.c interest. 
we then determine materiality under the
appropriate regulation in Sections L8702.2 through L8702'6'
(Regulation La702 t copy enclosed.)
Mr. Kennedy's econornic interest, Eschaton, is not directly
involved in the decisions by the city council. 
we therefore turn
to the analysis of indirect effects of Mr. Kennedy's decisions on
Eschaton. Regulation LA702-5 (copy enclosed) provides.the test
for determining whether the indirect effect of a decision on a
nonprofit entiiy is rnaterial. For Eschaton, the effect of a
decision is naterial if:
(d) For an entity whose gross annual receipts
are more than $1,OOO,OOO, but Iess than or equal to
$1o,ooo,ooo the effect of the decision will be any
of the following:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease of the entity's gross annual receipts
for a fiscal year in the amount of $100,000 or
more.
(2) The decision will cause the entity to
incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in
the anount of 525,000 or more-


our File No.
Page 5
I-90-775
(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the entity's assets or
liabilities 
in the amount of $100,00o or more'
Regulation 1-87 02 ,5
Should the foreseeable cost of foreclosure be S25,0oo or
more, Eschaton vrould be materially affected by decisions of Mr'
i.""!av. 
while the Eschaton loan is nore than adequately secured
fV tt"-pt"perty 
and Eschaton would likeLy recoup its expenses in
"i,V 
-i".^""f '"sur-e sale of the property, the f oreclosure would result
in some expenses nonetheless- and such expenses should be evaluated
p"t="""t-t-" negufation La7o2.5(d) (2) ' 
You should also examine the
effect on the gross receipts of bsciraton should the city council
ai=.pp.or" the Eschaton l6an project and cHC fail- to rnake interest
;;6;;a=. 
while any loss in interest payrnents $'outd be- recouped
[ni."gt 
any sale of the ProPerty, you shoutd consider whether any
a.iuv-i-" tire collection 6r inteiest pa)ments would cause an effect
in tie amounts set forth in subdivision (d) (1) of Regulation
ta102-5.
D. 
Public GeneraIlv
Even if the reasonably foreseeabte f j-nancial effect of a
aecision ii material, disqual if ication is required only. if the
.ii""t- i=-aistinguishable frorn the effect upon the public-
g"""." fiy. --i s""€i"" 87103.) If the decision does not affect all
ihe rnembLrs of the public in trre same manner, disqua l if ication may
u.-."q;i."a 
unLess the effect of the decision is the same as the
"ir."t-"" 
a significant 
segrment of the pubric. 
(Regulation 18703,
copy enclosed. )
The effect of the decision upon Eschaton will- be different
t.orn in" p"nfi" generally. 
Eschaton vill 
be affected uniquely as
;;;-"; 
inL creaiiors of Lhe cuc' we doubt that many' if any'
other residents of Santa cruz would be affected in the sane manner
as Eschaton.
In sum, should the disapproval of the Eschaton loan proiect
.u,r="-n."nifon to incur additional expenses of $25'00o or more or
=n."ia-in" decision cause an increase or decrease of Eschaton's
;;;;;*";;;"i-i-ceipts 
for the fiscar vear to be $100'00o or nore'
ur. Kennedy woura riieiy t'u'" a conf l-ict of interest regarding
I""i"i""= 
loncerning the sschaton loan project'
The analysis on decisions concerning other cHc proiects v,ouId
fof f ow--ifre saio" tesi=. again tne. deterninative questions -are
whether Mr. Kennedy i= 
- a""iii""s will have a foreseeable effect
upon Mr. Kennedy'= =ot'i"" of incorne ' Eschaton' and whether that
effect witl be ."a..Ili-"r- 
=p"-if i"i . in the above discussion on
;;;;;i"tray- 
wiLr t'ti'-x"t't'"ay's decisions on the other cHc
projects have an "ri""t^'I""cn'c'= tririty to pay the Eschaton loan?
2.


our FiIe No-
Page 6
I-90-775
we are unable to determine from the facts provided to us what
effect decisions by Mr. Kennedy on other cHc projects will- have on
the ability of cHc to repay the Eschaton loan. If, for example,
the city council's disapproval of another cHc project could reduce
the arnount of additional monies CHC may have available to pay the
Eschaton l-oan or cause cHc to incur nore liability 
that would rnake
it difficult 
for cEc to pay the Eschaton loan, then Mr- Kennedy
may al,so have a conflict of interest on votes concerning other cHc
proj ects .
No conflict of Interest
Under the same analysis, should Eschaton no longer be a
creditor of CHc, fron the facts presented, l1r. Kennedy would no
longer have a conflict of interest in decisions concerning cHc.
Any decisions on cHc projects would have no forseeable effect on
Eslhaton since cHc would have no obligation to pay off any I'oans
to Eschaton.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please contact ne at (916) 322-59oL-
Sincerely,
Scott HaIlabrin
Acting General Counsel-
